Domains.Name: domain-bit-calculator-arithmetic
Domains.Title: Gate-Level Bit Calculator
Domains.Description: 4-bit arithmetic (add/sub/mul/div) computed by a Rosetta-style logic-gate netlist. A federated provider whose full proof model lives in domains/natural-number-arithmetic/bit-calculator/. Nothing arithmetic is stored -- only input bits; every result bit is a fixed-point computation over gate truth tables. Second, independent zero-import witness of the four flagship arithmetic facts.

Theorems.Title: Fermat's Last Theorem
Theorems.Statement: For every integer n>2, there are no nonzero integers x,y,z with x^n+y^n=z^n.
Theorems.Scope: The current instrument models the prime-exponent p>=5 Frey/Wiles/Ribet route and its reductions.
Theorems.TrustBoundary: Seven foundation provider theorems remain imported in v21.
Theorems.NextTarget: Replace provider imports one theorem repository at a time while rebuilding the complete theorem graph.

Theorems.Title: Two Plus Two Equals Four
Theorems.Scope: Closed derivation over Zero/Successor using add-zero-left and add-succ-left only; no imported theorem.
Theorems.TrustBoundary: PeanoNaturalNumberKernel: Zero, Successor, and the recursive operation definitions are the trusted base.
Theorems.NextTarget: Generalize to associativity and commutativity of addition.

Theorems.Title: Four Minus Two Equals Two
Theorems.Statement: NaturalSub(4,2) = 2 in the Peano naturals (truncated subtraction).
Theorems.Scope: Closed derivation using sub-n-zero and sub-succ-succ only; subtraction is total via truncation at zero; no imported theorem.
Theorems.TrustBoundary: PeanoNaturalNumberKernel: Zero, Successor, and the recursive operation definitions are the trusted base.
Theorems.NextTarget: Characterize truncated subtraction as the left adjoint of addition on the naturals.

Theorems.Title: Two Times Two Equals Four
Theorems.Scope: Closed derivation using mul-zero-left and mul-succ-left, reducing to the already-internalized addition; no imported theorem.
Theorems.TrustBoundary: PeanoNaturalNumberKernel: Zero, Successor, and the recursive operation definitions are the trusted base.
Theorems.NextTarget: Generalize to distributivity of multiplication over addition.

Theorems.Title: Four Divided By Two Equals Two
Theorems.Statement: NaturalDiv(4,2) = 2 in the Peano naturals (exact division; partial where the divisor does not divide the dividend).
Theorems.Scope: Closed derivation using div-base and div-sub-step (repeated truncated subtraction); exact because the remainder reaches zero; no imported theorem.
Theorems.TrustBoundary: PeanoNaturalNumberKernel: Zero, Successor, and the recursive operation definitions are the trusted base.
Theorems.NextTarget: Model the remainder and prove the division algorithm 4 = 2*q + r with 0<=r<2.

Theorems.Name: bit-calculator-4bit-arithmetic
Theorems.Title: Gate-Level 4-Bit Arithmetic
Theorems.Statement: The four elementary operations on 4-bit naturals, each computed by a logic-gate netlist; the four flagship cases settle to the Peano results.
Theorems.Scope: 4-bit unsigned add/sub/mul/div by netlist; width is a data parameter. Nothing arithmetic is stored -- only input bits are data.
Theorems.NextTarget: 8-bit width; signed arithmetic; a small calculator UI over vw_computation_answer; python/xlsx witness substrates.

ProofFacts.Notes: E[3] is irreducible or reducible, exclusively.
Finite image, Brauer traces, determinant, and oddness.
X0(15)(Q) consists of eight explicitly enumerated points.
The four noncuspidal points have conductor 50 and are not semistable.
A semistable curve cannot correspond to a noncuspidal X0(15) point.
The disjoint p-component rank-p scheme gives the middle extension.
The valuation criterion is proved from K*/K*^p.
G12 over Z[sqrt(-2)] reduces bijectively to GL2(F3).
Restrict the full G12 lift to the actual residual image subgroup.
The characteristic-zero finite-image representation is assembled.
The p=5,7 Frey point specializes to the distinguished cusp.
Formal immersion forces the noncuspidal Frey point to be a cusp, contradiction.
Finite thin loci and local residue constraints.
Parent theorem derived from finite compiler plus kernel.
A rational p-isogeny gives a cyclic 2p-isogeny; all small cases are excluded.
For p>11, 2p is not an allowed rational cyclic-isogeny degree.
The Frey irreducibility parent is derived.
The auxiliary curve satisfies the required local and generic properties.
The weight-one automorphic representation is assembled.
The A2 theta series is a weight-one form of character chi_-3.
All nonconstant coefficients are multiples of 6.
The residual trace comes from the already irreducible residual representation.
Theorem 4.26 applicability is split into good and residual-prime cases.
Semistable local inertia supplies the required elementary-unipotent profile.
Upper and lower transvections generate SL2.
Cyclotomic determinant upgrades SL2 to GL2.
Multiplication and packet projection preserve the residual Hecke system.
The irreducible mod-3 branch is modular of weight two.
Complete class table and Frobenius factorization classifier.
Parent theorem derived from finite compiler plus kernel.
No realized branch facts are asserted.
Good, ordinary, finite-flat schema, and Steinberg models.
Every finite class has unramified Frobenius representatives.
The old compatibility parent is derived.
Perfect q-expansion duality gives a one-dimensional m-torsion line.
Independent evaluation vectors and desired finite quotient classes are constructed.
Analytic Chebotarev supplies primes in the selected classes.
Free cover, Shapiro, and involution contract.
D(V)^0 has dimension one and D(V) has twice that dimension.
The parent theorem is derived without asserting either branch as realized.
Rational rank plus residue dimension gives freeness.
Same rank-two compiler at auxiliary level.
Complementary projectors and one-dimensional residue signs.
Unit trace bases, Cayley–Hamilton quotients, and matrix units.
Compatible finite-stage representations form the completed representation.
The omnibus parent is assembled from two child theorem inputs and an internal compiler.
Traces, determinants, and local U operators generate T.
Finite bounds, diagonal selection, and inverse limit.
The algebraic patching theorem is no longer imported.
A positive-rank free R-module has zero annihilator.
The kernel annihilates a faithful module, so it is zero.
All finite certificate-generator interfaces are present.
S(m)+n = S(m+n): pull one successor out.
Pull the second successor out: S(S(0+S(S(Z)))).
0+n = n: inner term becomes S(S(Z)), giving S(S(S(S(Z)))).
S(S(S(S(Z)))) is the normal form of numeral 4. QED.
S(m)-S(n) = m-n: cancel one successor each side.
Cancel the second successor: S(S(Z)) - Z.
S(S(Z)) is the normal form of numeral 2. QED.
Reduce the additions via internalized addition: 2 + 2 = 4.
Normal form S(S(S(S(Z)))) is numeral 4. QED.
S(S(Z)) is the normal form of numeral 2. QED.

Loops.Title: Equation space
Loops.DomainQuestion: What counts as a nontrivial integer solution of x^n + y^n = z^n?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Positive controls for n=2 and near-misses for n>2 are stored as explicit rows.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add signed and projective normalizations.

Loops.Title: Exponent reduction
Loops.DomainQuestion: Why is it enough to handle exponent 4 and odd prime exponents?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Composite exponents reduce to a prime divisor; powers of two reduce to exponent 4.
Loops.NextFrontier: Machine-check reduction paths for arbitrary n.

Loops.Title: Primitive normalization
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can every counterexample be divided to gcd(a,b,c)=1?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Primitive normalization and pairwise-coprimality rules are registered.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add ideal-theoretic normalization later.

Loops.Title: Signed parity normalization
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can a primitive odd-prime case be relabeled so one signed term is even?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The signed equation a^p+b^p+c^p=0 permits relabeling with a even and b,c odd.
Loops.NextFrontier: Complete the exact 2-adic congruence normalization.

Loops.Title: Bounded search harness
Loops.DomainQuestion: What does direct enumeration witness, and where does it stop being proof?
Loops.WitnessSummary: n=2 positive controls appear; n=3,4,5,7 have no solutions in the tested box.
Loops.NextFrontier: Scale or replace with modular/local sieves.

Loops.Title: Frey construction
Loops.DomainQuestion: What curve is forced by a hypothetical prime-exponent solution?
Loops.WitnessSummary: E: y^2=x(x-a^p)(x+b^p) is generated from sample parameters.
Loops.NextFrontier: Attach symbolic hypothetical-solution rows.

Loops.Title: Weierstrass coefficients
Loops.DomainQuestion: What are a1,a2,a3,a4,a6 after expanding the cubic?
Loops.NextFrontier: Add change-of-model transformations.

Loops.Title: Curve invariants
Loops.DomainQuestion: Do Delta, c4, c6 and j derive from the same upstream powers?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Symbolic identities and c4^3-c6^2=1728 Delta pass.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add minimal discriminant models.

Loops.Title: Rational 2-torsion
Loops.DomainQuestion: Which rational points are visible before any advanced theory?
Loops.WitnessSummary: (0,0), (a^p,0), (-b^p,0) lie on every sampled curve.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add explicit group-law doubling witness.

Loops.Title: Bad-prime support
Loops.DomainQuestion: Which odd primes can make the Frey model singular?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Odd bad primes are exactly among divisors of a*b*(a^p+b^p) in samples.
Loops.NextFrontier: Resolve the prime 2 separately.

Loops.Title: Odd-prime reduction type
Loops.DomainQuestion: At odd bad primes, is c4 a unit while Delta vanishes?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Every sampled odd bad prime has v_q(c4)=0 and v_q(Delta)>0.
Loops.NextFrontier: Connect to a formal Tate-algorithm certificate.

Loops.Title: Odd conductor
Loops.DomainQuestion: What conductor exponent follows from multiplicative reduction?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Each sampled odd bad prime receives conductor exponent 1.
Loops.NextFrontier: Derive the exact global conductor after the 2-adic loop.

Loops.Title: Discriminant p-divisibility
Loops.DomainQuestion: Why do odd discriminant valuations disappear modulo p under the FLT relation?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Divisors of a or b have observed valuations divisible by p; the c-side is encoded conditionally on a^p+b^p=c^p.
Loops.NextFrontier: Formalize residual unramifiedness from valuation data.

Loops.Title: Two-adic frontier
Loops.DomainQuestion: What exact integral model controls reduction and conductor at 2?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Resolved in loops 22–26: normalized integral model, exact minimal discriminant, c4 unit at 2, multiplicative reduction, and conductor exponent 1 are all witnessed.
Loops.NextFrontier: Decompose the residual finiteness criterion and imported global theorems.

Loops.Title: Residual representation
Loops.DomainQuestion: How is E[p] represented as a Galois object in the dependency graph?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The object and its required properties are explicit theorem nodes.
Loops.NextFrontier: Connect to a computer algebra system for concrete curves.

Loops.Title: Irreducibility gate
Loops.DomainQuestion: What blocks a proper invariant line in E[p] for p>3?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The load-bearing result is isolated as an imported theorem edge rather than hidden.
Loops.NextFrontier: Pull apart the Mazur/isogeny argument.

Loops.Title: Semistable modularity gate
Loops.DomainQuestion: What turns the Frey curve into a modular curve?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Wiles and Taylor-Wiles are represented as sourced imported theorem nodes.
Loops.NextFrontier: Decompose residual modularity and R=T machinery.

Loops.Title: Level lowering
Loops.DomainQuestion: How do the odd bad primes disappear from the residual level?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Ribet's implication is represented as a sourced conjunction rule.
Loops.NextFrontier: Witness local hypotheses one prime at a time.

Loops.Title: Level-2 obstruction
Loops.DomainQuestion: Is there a weight-2 cusp form at level 2 to receive the representation?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The genus formula gives g(X0(2))=dim S2(Gamma0(2))=0.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add q-expansion and Hecke-algebra witnesses.

Loops.Title: Contradiction closure
Loops.DomainQuestion: Does the contradiction emerge from the graph rather than being stored?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Forward chaining derives both existence and nonexistence of a level-2 newform.
Loops.NextFrontier: Replace imported edges progressively with finer witnessed subgraphs.

Loops.Title: Epistemic health
Loops.DomainQuestion: Which edges are algebraic, computational, conditional, imported, or unresolved?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Every check is tiered; the 2-adic machine proof is explicitly NOT_EVALUATED.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add proof-assistant certificates per edge.

Loops.Title: Residue normalization at 2
Loops.DomainQuestion: Does every primitive odd-prime Fermat residue class admit a permutation with b even and a ≡ -1 mod 4?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Exhaustive modulo-32 enumeration for p=5,7,11,13 yields 3,072 qualifying classes; every class has exactly one even term and normalizes successfully.
Loops.NextFrontier: Use the normalization to construct the integral minimal Frey model.

Loops.Title: Integral 2-adic change of variables
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can the nonminimal cubic be transformed into an integral Weierstrass model with discriminant divided by 2^12?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The change x=4X, y=4X+8Y is symbolically verified; all sampled normalized models have integral coefficients.
Loops.NextFrontier: Prove minimality and multiplicative reduction at 2 from c4.

Loops.Title: Minimality and multiplicative reduction at 2
Loops.DomainQuestion: Does the transformed model have c4 a 2-adic unit and positive discriminant valuation?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Every normalized sample has v2(c4)=0 and v2(Delta)>0, forcing a minimal multiplicative model at 2.
Loops.NextFrontier: Combine with odd-prime local geometry into a global model.

Loops.Title: Global minimal discriminant
Loops.DomainQuestion: Is Delta_min=2^-8(ABC)^2 minimal at every prime under the Frey normalization?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Prime-by-prime c4-unit checks pass at every bad prime of every normalized sample.
Loops.NextFrontier: Derive the exact conductor radical.

Loops.Title: Global conductor radical
Loops.DomainQuestion: Are the bad primes exactly the divisors of ABC, each with conductor exponent one?
Loops.WitnessSummary: All sampled bad primes, including 2, are multiplicative; sample conductors equal rad(ABC).
Loops.NextFrontier: Translate minimal-discriminant valuations into residual finiteness.

Loops.Title: Residual finite/nonfinite profile
Loops.DomainQuestion: Which primes are finite for E[p], and which prime anchors level lowering?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Formal FLT valuation profiles make every odd prime finite and prime 2 nonfinite because v2(Delta_min) ≡ -8 mod p.
Loops.NextFrontier: Derive the residual level coordinates.

Loops.Title: Residual level and determinant coordinates
Loops.DomainQuestion: What level, weight, determinant, and local statuses characterize the Frey residual representation?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The graph now separates derived valuation geometry from the sourced Weil-pairing and finite-flat criteria.
Loops.NextFrontier: Witness Frobenius traces on concrete curves.

Loops.Title: Frobenius point-count engine
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can traces of Frobenius be derived directly from point counts over finite fields?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Point counts are computed for every normalized sample at many good primes; characteristic polynomials are stored.
Loops.NextFrontier: Check Hasse bounds and determinant coordinates.

Loops.Title: Hasse and determinant invariants
Loops.DomainQuestion: Do all computed traces satisfy |a_q|≤2√q and det(Frob_q)=q?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Every point-count row satisfies the Hasse bound; determinant and trace residues are explicit.
Loops.NextFrontier: Build the coefficient interface to modular forms.

Loops.Title: Euler and Hecke recurrence interface
Loops.DomainQuestion: What coefficient relations distinguish a normalized eigenform from an arbitrary sequence?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Prime-square, coprime multiplicativity, and bad-prime recurrences are checked on the level-11 eta product.
Loops.NextFrontier: Construct a concrete elliptic-curve/modular-form pair.

Loops.Title: Concrete semistable curve of conductor 11
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can the curve side of modularity be witnessed on a small nontrivial example?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The curve y^2+y=x^3-x^2-10x-20 has Delta=-11^5, c4=496, and conductor 11 by the same local geometry.
Loops.NextFrontier: Generate the corresponding q-series independently.

Loops.Title: Eta-product q-series at level 11
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can a candidate weight-2 level-11 coefficient sequence be generated without a modular-form black box?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The product q∏(1-q^n)^2(1-q^(11n))^2 is expanded through q^250.
Loops.NextFrontier: Witness the eta-quotient modularity criteria.

Loops.Title: Eta-quotient criterion witness
Loops.DomainQuestion: Do the level, weight, character, and cusp-order arithmetic line up for eta(z)^2 eta(11z)^2?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Both congruence sums equal 24, the character product is a square, and both cusp orders equal 1.
Loops.NextFrontier: Compare coefficients with curve Frobenius traces.

Loops.Title: Curve-form trace matching
Loops.DomainQuestion: Do independently generated curve traces and eta-product coefficients agree at good primes?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Every tested prime q<200, q≠11 matches exactly.
Loops.NextFrontier: Reduce the equality modulo residual primes.

Loops.Title: Residual congruence witnesses
Loops.DomainQuestion: What does it mean computationally for E[p] to arise from a modular form?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Trace congruences are checked modulo p=5,7,13 at all tested good Frobenius primes.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add a finite certification bound.

Loops.Title: Sturm finite-certification boundary
Loops.DomainQuestion: How many q-coefficients can certify equality once both objects are known modular?
Loops.WitnessSummary: The level-11 weight-2 Sturm bound is 2; the database records the bound separately from the modularity assumptions.
Loops.NextFrontier: Execute the level-lowering cascade.

Loops.Title: Conditional level-lowering cascade
Loops.DomainQuestion: Given modularity, irreducibility, and the finite/nonfinite profile, does every odd prime disappear from the level?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Four formal profiles remove every odd prime one at a time and terminate at level 2 while retaining the nonfinite anchor at 2.
Loops.NextFrontier: Map the terminal modular-form space.

Loops.Title: Small-level modular-form phase diagram
Loops.DomainQuestion: Where does weight-2 cusp-form space first become nonzero as level increases?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Levels 1–10 have dimension zero; level 11 is the first positive point. Level 2 is visibly deep inside the zero basin.
Loops.NextFrontier: Audit which theorem edges remain load-bearing.

Loops.Title: Dependency cutsets and counterfactual builds
Loops.DomainQuestion: Which imported rules are individually necessary for the contradiction in the current graph?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Each main imported gate is disabled in turn; every such counterfactual build loses the contradiction.
Loops.NextFrontier: Quantify the remaining import surface.

Loops.Title: Import surface and next proof frontier

Loops.Title: Natural-number addition: 2+2=4
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can 2+2=4 be derived from Zero/Successor without importing its own conclusion?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Five-step reduction S(S(0))+S(S(0)) -> S(S(S(S(0)))) via add-succ-left x2 and add-zero-left; recognized as numeral 4.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add truncated subtraction as the first partial operation.

Loops.Title: Natural-number subtraction: 4-2=2
Loops.DomainQuestion: Does truncated subtraction stay total on the naturals while remaining definitionally exact for 4-2?
Loops.WitnessSummary: Four-step reduction cancelling successors pairwise then n-0=n; normal form S(S(0)) = numeral 2.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add multiplication, reusing internalized addition.

Loops.Title: Natural-number multiplication: 2*2=4
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can multiplication reduce through the already-internalized addition rather than re-importing it?
Loops.WitnessSummary: mul-succ-left x2 and mul-zero-left expand 2*2 to 2+(2+0), which reduces to 4 via internalized addition.
Loops.NextFrontier: Add exact division as repeated truncated subtraction.

Loops.Title: Natural-number division: 4/2=2
Loops.DomainQuestion: Can exact division be witnessed as repeated truncated subtraction that terminates when the remainder hits zero?
Loops.WitnessSummary: div-sub-step x2 reach a zero dividend (base case), quotient increments 1+1+0 = numeral 2; reuses internalized subtraction.
Loops.NextFrontier: Model remainder r and the division algorithm 4 = 2q + r; then close the domain in the cleanup loop.
